elements.sent-2411-001: This paper provides formulae and algorithms to compute the projection onto the core of a preimputation outside it.
elements.sent-2411-002: The core of a game is described using an exponential number of linear constraints, and we cannot know beforehand which are redundant or defining the polytope.
elements.sent-2411-003: We apply these new results to market games, a class of games in which every game has a nonempty core.
elements.sent-2411-004: Given an initial state of the game represented by a preimputation, it is not guaranteed that the state of the game evolves toward the core following the dynamics induced by the domination relations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results identify and compute the most efficient side payment that acts on a given state of the game and yields its closest core allocation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Using this side payment, we propose a way to evaluate the failure of a market to reach a state of the economy belonging to the core, and we propose a new solution concept consisting of preimputations that minimizes this failure.
elements.sent-2411-007: The first solution concept introduced for solving cooperative games are the stable sets, which, according to \textcite{von1944theory , model the standards of behavior of the social context represented by the game under consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The point was to ``embrace the phenomena of social equilibrium and changes'' and ``the idea of capturing social configurations'' (\textcite{leonard2010neumann ).}
elements.sent-2411-009: Rapidly after, the complementary solution concept of the core is introduced by Shapley (\textcite{zhao2018three, shubik1992game ), and its relation with the stable sets studied by \textcite{gillies1959solutions .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The core is defined as the polytope, possibly empty, of all allocations of the grand coalition revenue to all players such that no coalition can provide a better allocation for all its members.
elements.sent-2411-011: The property of having a nonempty core is an important one for a game, because ``a game that has a core has less potential for social conflict than one without a core'' (\textcite{shubik1982game ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, the core can be thought of as the set of socially fair allocations, while the stable sets represent the natural social order of the social context modeled by the game under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-013: Even for a game with a nonempty core, if this core is not a stable set, nothing ensures that the distribution of the players' endowments evolves towards a fair state.
elements.sent-2411-014: To solve this issue, we propose formulae and algorithms to compute the most efficient reallocation of players' endowments.
elements.sent-2411-015: Given any initial state represented by a preimputation, that is, an allocation that distributes the whole revenue of the grand coalition among all the players, we are able to compute the closest core allocation.
elements.sent-2411-016: This redistribution of the players' endowments does not rely on the properties of the stable sets, but on an external force or arbitrator.
elements.sent-2411-017: More formally, we compute for any preimputation outside the core its projection onto the core.
elements.sent-2411-018: According to the Hilbert Projection Theorem, the projection of an allocation outside the core onto it is the closest preimputation in the core from the initial allocation.
elements.sent-2411-019: The task of projecting onto the core is difficult, because the H-description of the core requires an exponential number of values, and we don't know in advance which of these values are relevant.
elements.sent-2411-020: An important tool we use is a projector onto some specific affine subspaces of the space of allocations, for which exact formulae are provided.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, we give an algorithm which associates, to any preimputation, a collection of coalitions for which the projection that gives a payment exactly equal to their value belongs to the core.
elements.sent-2411-022: Finally, we use these formulas and algorithms to compute the distance between a preimputation and the core of a game, and propose a map which associates to each preimputation a value measuring the failure of this given preimputation to be in the core, which we apply to market games.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let \(N\) be finite set of \(n\) players.
elements.sent-2411-024: We want to know whether the players in \(N\) would agree to fully cooperate.
elements.sent-2411-025: For that to be the case, forming the coalition \(N\) must be in the interest of every player.
elements.sent-2411-026: In particular, there should not be any subcoalition of \(N\) able to provide to its players more than what \(N\) can provide to them.
elements.sent-2411-027: To formally quantify this idea, we use a coalitional function, a set function \(v\) from the power set \(2^N\) of \(N\) to the real numbers \( \mathbb{R \), which associates to each subset \(S\) of \(N\) a real number \(v(S)\), called the worth of \(S\).}
elements.sent-2411-028: We can interpret the worth of \(S\) as the amount of utility that the players in \(S\) have by cooperating during one unit of time, and that they can freely allocate among themselves.
elements.sent-2411-029: The nonempty subsets of \(N\) are called coalitions, and their set is denoted by \( \mathcal{N \).}
elements.sent-2411-030: As previously discussed, a necessary condition for the grand coalition to form is that it must be able to distribute more utility, assuming it is transferable, among the players than any of its subcoalitions can.
elements.sent-2411-031: Denote by \(X(v)\) the affine hyperplane defined by \begin{equation}X(v) = \{x \in R^N \mid x(N) = v(N)\},\end{equation} where \(x(S) = \sum_{i \in S x_i\) for any coalition \(S \subseteq N\) and \(\mathbb{R ^N\) denotes the \(n\)-fold Cartesian product of \(n\) copies of \( \mathbb{R \), one for each player.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: For any player \(i \in N\), the coordinate \(x_i\) represents the payment of \(i\) by \(x\).
elements.sent-2411-033: The payment of a coalition \(S\) is simply the sum \(x(S)\) of the payment of the players.
elements.sent-2411-034: We denote by \(\mathbf{1 ^i\) the \(i\)-th vector of the canonical base of \(\mathbb{R ^N\), i.e., the vector giving a payment of \(1\) to player \(i\) and \(0\) to the other players.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We denote by \(\mathbf{1 ^S\) the sum of vectors \(\mathbf{1 ^S \coloneqq \sum_{i \in S \mathbf{1 ^i\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The set \(X(v)\) represents all the possible ways to allocate \(v(N)\) among \(n\) players.
elements.sent-2411-037: The elements of \(X(v)\) are called the preimputations of the game \((N, v)\), and they represent the possible states of a given game.
elements.sent-2411-038: Therefore, the question is whether there exist some preimputations such that the payment of any coalition by these preimputations is better than what \(S\) can achieve on its own.
elements.sent-2411-039: In other words, we want to know whether the following set \begin{equation}C(v) = \{x \in X(v) \mid x(S) \geq v(S), \forall S \in N \},\end{equation} called the core of the game \((N, v)\), is nonempty.
elements.sent-2411-040: This question was solved by \textcite{bondareva1963some and \textcite{shapley1967balanced independently, using the concept of balanced collections.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: A balanced collection \( \mathcal{B \) models a social configuration of the players in \(N\).}
elements.sent-2411-042: They represent how the players can be organized, respecting two natural conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-043: The players forming a coalition spend the same time in it,.

elements.section-2411-003: The geometry of a cooperative game
elements.sent-2411-085: This section focuses on the affine subspace \( X(v) \subseteq \mathbb{R ^N \).}
elements.sent-2411-086: The linear subspace of \( \mathbb{R ^N \) parallel to \( X(v) \) is denoted by \( \Sigma \), and its elements are called the side payments.}
elements.sent-2411-087: A side payment represents a translation in the space of preimputations, which is a redistribution of utility between players, without changing the total amount acquired by the players.
elements.sent-2411-088: Indeed, if \( X(v) \) is defined by \( X(v) = \{ x \in \mathbb{R ^N \mid x(N) = v(N) \} \), then \( \Sigma = \{ x \in \mathbb{R ^N \mid x(N) = 0 \} \) is a vector space.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Also, notice that \( \Sigma \) does not depend on a given game, but only on the number of players, which defines its dimension.
elements.sent-2411-090: Because \( N \) is just an arbitrary coalition, we want to have the same subspaces in hands for smaller coalitions.
elements.sent-2411-092: We denote by \( A_S(v) \) the set of preimputations for which \( S\) exactly gets its value, i.e., \begin{equation}A_S(v) \coloneqq \{ x \in X(v) \mid x(S) = v(S) \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-093: The linear subspace of \( \mathbb{R ^N \) parallel to \( A_S(v) \) is denoted by \( H_S \), is a linear subspace of \( \Sigma \) as well, and is defined by \( H_S = \{\sigma \in \Sigma \mid \sigma(S) = 0 \} \).}
elements.sent-2411-094: These two sets of affine subspaces define hyperplane arrangements, respectively in \( X(v) \) and \( \Sigma \).
elements.sent-2411-095: The hyperplane arrangement formed by \( \{H_S \mid S \in \mathcal{N \} \) is known as the resonance arrangement, and does not depend on the game under consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-096: However, the hyperplane arrangement formed by \( \{ A_S(v) \mid S \in \mathcal{N \} \) is a distortion of the resonance arrangement that depends on the game.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Hence, its chambers, i.e., the connected components of the complement of \( \{ A_S(v) \mid S \in \mathcal{N \} \), summarize the game by containing the right amount of information about it, especially about the core, and domination between preimputations.}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Finding a minimal reaching collection} \label{algo: find-reach-coll} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require A balanced game \( (N, v) \), a preimputation \( x \) \Ensure A minimal reaching collection \State Find \( \mathcal{Q} \) such that \( x \in X_\mathcal{Q}(v) \) \State Initialize \( \mathcal{K} \gets \{\{S\} \mid S \in \mathcal{Q} \} \) \For{\( \mathcal{T} \in \mathcal{K} \)} \If{\( \max_{S} e_S(\pi_{A_\mathcal{T}}(x)) \leq 0 \)} \State return \( \mathcal{T} \) \Else \For{\( S \in \mathcal{Q} \)} \If{\( \mathcal{T} \cup \{S\} \not \in \mathcal{K} \) and \Call{UpdateGramian}{$ \mathcal{T}, S $} \( \neq 0 \)} \State Add \( \mathcal{T} \cup \{S\} \) to \( \mathcal{K} \) \EndIf \EndFor \State Remove \( \mathcal{T} \) from \( \mathcal{K} \) \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
